main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Development of NT-Java-1.1B, a specialized code language model for Java based on StarCoderBase-1.1B","Fine-tuning and quantization of the base model for Java-specific tasks","Evaluation on MultiPL-E Java code benchmark"],"problems":["Lack of small, specialized code models for programming languages other than Python","High hardware requirements and inference costs of large code models, making them unsuitable for desktop deployment","Generic multi-lingual code models not meeting nuanced, language-specific requirements for enterprise coding tasks"],"datasets":["MultiPL-E Java code benchmark"],"metrics":["Performance on MultiPL-E Java code benchmark"],"results":[{"metric":"MultiPL-E Java code benchmark (relative to similar-sized models)","value":"State-of-the-art performance; surpasses base model and majority of other 1.1B models"}],"novelty_claims":["First open-source, specialized Java code language model at 1.1B parameter scale for desktop deployment","Demonstrates that small, specialized code models can achieve state-of-the-art performance on Java code tasks","Establishes a foundation for a family of Narrow Transformer models specialized by language and size"]}

methods: Development of DeepSeek-Coder series (open-source code LLMs) with sizes from 1.3B to 33B parameters
Training from scratch on 2 trillion tokens from 87 programming languages
Pre-training on high-quality project-level (repository-level) code corpus
Use of Fill-In-Middle (FIM) approach for code completion and infilling
Extension of context window to 16K tokens for handling longer code inputs
Fine-tuning with instructional data to create DeepSeek-Coder-Instruct models
problems: Performance gap between open-source and closed-source code LLMs
Limited accessibility and research opportunities due to closed-source code models
Need for models capable of understanding and generating code across multiple programming languages and large codebases
Improving code completion and infilling capabilities in LLMs
Handling longer code contexts in code generation tasks
datasets: 2 trillion tokens of code from 87 programming languages (custom, high-quality project-level corpus)
Various public code-related benchmarks (specific names not provided in the excerpt)
metrics: Performance on code-related benchmarks (e.g., pass@1, accuracy, etc. as typical in code LLM evaluation, though not explicitly named in the excerpt)

results.metric: Performance on code-related benchmarks (DeepSeek-Coder-Base 33B vs open-source models)
results.value: DeepSeek-Coder-Base 33B achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source code models across all benchmarks

results.metric: Performance on code-related benchmarks (DeepSeek-Coder-Instruct 33B vs OpenAI GPT-3.5 Turbo)
results.value: DeepSeek-Coder-Instruct 33B surpasses OpenAI GPT-3.5 Turbo in the majority of evaluation benchmarks
DeepSeek-Coder-Base 7B demonstrates competitive performance compared to CodeLlama-33B, despite having five times fewer parameters

novelty_claims: Introduction of DeepSeek-Coder, a series of open-source code LLMs trained from scratch on a massive, diverse code corpus
First attempt to incorporate repository-level data construction during pre-training to enhance cross-file code generation
Comprehensive analysis of Fill-In-Middle (FIM) training strategies and their impact on code model pretraining
Release of models under a permissive license allowing unrestricted research and commercial use
DeepSeek-Coder-Instruct 33B narrows the performance gap between open-source models and OpenAI GPT-4, surpassing GPT-3.5 Turbo in most benchmarks

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Development of MonoCoder, a domain-specific language model for HPC code, by reducing the number of layers of PolyCoder and pretraining only on C and C++ code from the HPCORPUS dataset","Design and implementation of Local Semantics Elimination (LSE), a code preprocessing method to remove local semantics from code before training and evaluation","Empirical evaluation of MonoCoder against state-of-the-art LLMs (PolyCoder, GPT-3.5) on both general-purpose and HPC-specific programming tasks"],"problems":["Inefficiency and poor performance of large, general-purpose LLMs on HPC-specific programming tasks such as code parallelization and vectorization","High computational and financial cost of training large LLMs for domain-specific tasks","Over-reliance of existing code LMs on local code semantics, leading to degraded performance in code completion and generation tasks"],"datasets":["HPCORPUS: A dataset of C and C++ programs mined from GitHub, containing approximately 300k repositories, 70GB of code, 9 million files, and 155 million functions","A dataset of 20,000 OpenMP codes for parallel code generation evaluation"],"metrics":["Perplexity (normalized-perplexity)","CodeBLEU score"],"results":[{"metric":"Perplexity (code completion task)","value":"MonoCoder achieves comparable perplexity to PolyCoder despite being 4x smaller"},{"metric":"CodeBLEU (parallel code generation)","value":"MonoCoder outperforms PolyCoder and GPT-3.5 on CodeBLEU scores for HPC-specific tasks"},{"metric":"Performance with Local Semantics Elimination (LSE)","value":"MonoCoder outperforms other LMs in all code completion settings and context lengths, with and without LSE; other LMs degrade considerably with LSE"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of MonoCoder, a small, domain-specific language model for HPC code that is orders of magnitude smaller than existing LLMs but delivers better or comparable performance on HPC and non-HPC code tasks","Demonstration that training a language model from scratch on domain-specific data (C/C++ HPC code) is more effective for HPC tasks than finetuning large, general-purpose LLMs","Development of Local Semantics Elimination (LSE), a preprocessing method to remove local code semantics, improving model robustness and performance on code completion and generation tasks","Empirical evidence that MonoCoder outperforms state-of-the-art LLMs on normalized-perplexity and CodeBLEU for HPC code, especially under LSE"]}

methods: Development of MonoCoder, a domain-specific language model for HPC code, by reducing the number of layers of PolyCoder and pretraining only on C and C++ code from the HPCORPUS dataset
Design and implementation of Local Semantics Elimination (LSE), a code preprocessing method to remove local semantics from code before training and evaluation
Empirical evaluation of MonoCoder against state-of-the-art LLMs (PolyCoder, GPT-3.5) on both general-purpose and HPC-specific programming tasks
problems: Inefficiency and poor performance of large, general-purpose LLMs on HPC-specific programming tasks such as code parallelization and vectorization
High computational and financial cost of training large LLMs for domain-specific tasks
Over-reliance of existing code LMs on local code semantics, leading to degraded performance in code completion and generation tasks
datasets: HPCORPUS: A dataset of C and C++ programs mined from GitHub, containing approximately 300k repositories, 70GB of code, 9 million files, and 155 million functions
A dataset of 20,000 OpenMP codes for parallel code generation evaluation

results.value: MonoCoder achieves comparable perplexity to PolyCoder despite being 4x smaller
MonoCoder outperforms PolyCoder and GPT-3.5 on CodeBLEU scores for HPC-specific tasks
MonoCoder outperforms other LMs in all code completion settings and context lengths, with and without LSE; other LMs degrade considerably with LSE

novelty_claims: Introduction of MonoCoder, a small, domain-specific language model for HPC code that is orders of magnitude smaller than existing LLMs but delivers better or comparable performance on HPC and non-HPC code tasks
Demonstration that training a language model from scratch on domain-specific data (C/C++ HPC code) is more effective for HPC tasks than finetuning large, general-purpose LLMs
Development of Local Semantics Elimination (LSE), a preprocessing method to remove local code semantics, improving model robustness and performance on code completion and generation tasks
Empirical evidence that MonoCoder outperforms state-of-the-art LLMs on normalized-perplexity and CodeBLEU for HPC code, especially under LSE

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Cascade of training and fine-tuning steps starting from Llama 2 foundation models","Code-training from foundation models (Llama 2) on code and text data","Multitask objective combining autoregressive and causal infilling prediction for code infilling","Fine-tuning to extend context length up to 100,000 tokens by modifying RoPE positional embeddings","Instruction fine-tuning using proprietary instruction data and a machine-generated self-instruct dataset","Specialization into three main variants: Code Llama (general code), Code Llama - methods: Cascade of training and fine-tuning steps starting from Llama 2 foundation models

Multitask objective combining autoregressive and causal infilling prediction for code infilling

problems: Improving code generation performance of open-source large language models
Enabling code infilling (filling missing code segments with awareness of surrounding context)

results.value: Outperforms Llama 2 70B

All Code Llama models outperform every other publicly available model

novelty_claims: First open family of code-specialized Llama 2 models with state-of-the-art performance among open models on major code benchmarks

Extension of context length up to 100,000 tokens for code LLMs via RoPE embedding modification
Instruction-following code LLMs fine-tuned with both proprietary and self-instruct data for improved safety and helpfulness
Release under a permissive license allowing both research and commercial use

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Code Evol-Instruct: a novel instruction fine-tuning approach tailored for code, incorporating heuristics for coding tasks, adversarial sample heuristics, time/space complexity requirements, and evolving stop controls","Fine-tuning open-source Code LLMs (StarCoder, CodeLlama) with evolved, complex code instruction data to create WizardCoder models"],"problems":["Limited exploration of fine-grained instruction tuning in code large language models (Code LLMs)","Existing Code LLMs are primarily pre-trained on raw code data without complex instruction fine-tuning, limiting their performance on code generation tasks"],"datasets":["HumanEval","HumanEval+","MBPP","DS-1000","DS100","MultiPL-E","Code Alpaca (as base for instruction evolution)"],"metrics":["Pass rate (e.g., pass@1) on code generation benchmarks (HumanEval, HumanEval+, MBPP, DS-1000, MultiPL-E)"],"results":[{"metric":"HumanEval (pass@1)","value":"WizardCoder 34B achieves a score comparable to GPT3.5 (ChatGPT) and surpasses all open-source Code LLMs"},{"metric":"HumanEval+ (pass@1)","value":"WizardCoder 34B surpasses GPT3.5 (ChatGPT); selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Training a 1.3B parameter Transformer-based language model (phi-1) for code generation","Pretraining on 'textbook quality' data: 6B tokens filtered from the web and 1B tokens synthetically generated with GPT-3.5","Finetuning on a dataset of coding exercises ('textbook-exercise-like' data, <200M tokens)","Comparison with smaller (phi-1-small, 350M parameters) and base (phi-1-base) models to study emergent properties"],"problems":["Improving code generation performance of large language models with smaller model and dataset sizes","Breaking the traditional scaling laws by leveraging high-quality data instead of just increasing model or dataset size","Reducing the environmental and computational cost of training large language models for code"],"datasets":["HumanEval","MBPP (Mostly Basic Python Programs)","Textbook quality data (filtered from the web, 6B tokens)","Synthetic textbook and exercise data generated with GPT-3.5 (1B tokens)","Finetuning dataset of coding exercises (<200M tokens)"],"metrics":["pass@1 accuracy on HumanEval","pass@1 accuracy on MBPP"],"results":[{"metric":"HumanEval (pass@1)","value":"50.6%"},{"metric":"MBPP (pass@1)","value":"55.5%"},{"metric":"HumanEval (pass@1) for phi-1-small (350M parameters)","value":"45%"}],"novelty_claims":["Demonstrates that high-quality, 'textbook quality' data can break existing scaling laws, enabling small models to achieve state-of-the-art performance on code generation benchmarks","phi-1 achieves competitive or superior results to much larger models using orders of magnitude less data and compute","Shows that emergent properties can arise in small models when trained on high-quality data","First to show that high-quality data can improve SOTA of LLMs for code while dramatically reducing dataset size and training compute"]}

methods: Compressor: a genetic algorithm (GA)-guided model simplification approach for compressing pre-trained models of code
Knowledge distillation from large pre-trained models to small student models
Model architecture search constrained to similar designs as the original pre-trained model, guided by maximizing GFLOPs under a size constraint
problems: Large pre-trained models of code are too big (hundreds of MB) and slow for deployment on personal devices and in IDEs, causing high memory consumption and inference latency
Existing model compression techniques (pruning, quantization) are insufficient for reducing model size below practical thresholds (e.g., 3 MB) while maintaining performance and efficiency on CPUs
Difficulty in designing tiny student model architectures that retain enough capacity to absorb knowledge from large models during distillation

metrics: Accuracy (percentage of original model performance retained)

4.31x faster than original

96.95% of original
98.36% of original

results.metric: Accuracy loss reduction vs. BiLSTM_soft (vulnerability prediction)
Accuracy loss reduction vs. BiLSTM_soft (clone detection)
Time to find tiny model architecture
results.value: 1.22 seconds (average)
30.39% of fine-tuning time

novelty_claims: First to highlight and refine the problem of compressing pre-trained models of code as a model simplification process.
Proposes Compressor, a novel method combining genetic algorithm-guided model simplification and knowledge distillation for code model compression.
Demonstrates that pre-trained models of code can be compressed to 3 MB (160x smaller) with negligible performance loss and significant inference speedup on CPUs.
Shows that Compressor outperforms previous compression methods (e.g., BiLSTM_soft) in reducing accuracy loss.

methods: CODA (COde Difference guided Adversarial example generation): uses code differences between target and reference inputs to guide adversarial example generation, focusing on structure and identifier differences; applies equivalent structure transformations (e.g., for-to-while loop) and identifier renaming transformations; uses semantic similarity for identifier renaming; minimizes model invocations by only checking for fault-revealing examples, not using gradients or extensive model predictions
problems: Vulnerability of deep code models to adversarial attacks
Ineffectiveness and inefficiency of existing adversarial attack techniques for code due to large search space and discrete, grammar-constrained nature of code
Lack of naturalness in generated adversarial examples
High computational cost (frequent model invocations) in existing methods
datasets: Not explicitly named, but experiments conducted on 15 subjects across three pre-trained models (CodeBERT, GraphCodeBERT, CodeT5) and five code-based tasks
metrics: Reduction in faults after adversarial fine-tuning

results.metric: Attack success rate improvement over CARROT
results.value: 79.25% (average increase)

results.metric: Attack success rate improvement over ALERT
results.value: 72.20% (average increase)
88.05% more faults revealed (average across all subjects)
72.51% more faults revealed (average across all subjects)
196.96 hours (for 15 subjects)
290.87 hours (for 15 subjects)
374.51 hours (for 15 subjects)
62.19% reduction
65.67% reduction
73.95% reduction

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel perspective of using code differences between reference and target inputs to guide adversarial example generation for deep code models
Reduces the attack/search space by focusing only on code differences, improving effectiveness and efficiency
Minimizes model invocations by avoiding gradient calculations and extensive model predictions, improving efficiency
Ensures naturalness of generated adversarial examples by using semantic similarity in identifier renaming and structure-preserving transformations
Demonstrates superior effectiveness and efficiency over state-of-the-art techniques (CARROT, ALERT)

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Inputting exercise instructions from an undergraduate Java programming course into ChatGPT (versions 3.5 and 4) to generate code solutions","Executing the generated code on a local machine and comparing outputs to expected results","Analyzing the correctness, readability, and structure of ChatGPT-generated solutions"],"problems":["Evaluating the effectiveness of ChatGPT in generating correct solutions for undergraduate Java programming exercises","Assessing the potential and limitations of ChatGPT as a tool for students in programming education","Identifying risks of misuse of ChatGPT as a cheating aid in programming assessments"],"datasets":["80 diverse programming exercises from an undergraduate Java programming course"],"metrics":["Accuracy of generated solutions","Readability of code","Code structure/organization","Ability to generate alternative/memory-efficient solutions"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy of generated solutions","value":"ChatGPT accurately generates Java programming solutions for most exercises"},{"metric":"Readability and structure","value":"Generated solutions are highly readable and well-structured"},{"metric":"Ability to generate alternative solutions","value":"ChatGPT can produce alternative, memory-efficient solutions"},{"metric":"Handling of non-textual/class file exercises","value":"ChatGPT struggles and often produces invalid solutions"}],"novelty_claims":["First systematic evaluation of ChatGPT's effectiveness in generating solutions for a large set (80) of undergraduate Java programming exercises","Analysis of both strengths (accuracy, readability, alternative solutions) and limitations (handling non-textual/class file exercises) of ChatGPT in the context of programming education","Discussion of implications for educators regarding the design of coding exercises to minimize misuse of ChatGPT as a cheating tool"]}

methods: Inputting exercise instructions from an undergraduate Java programming course into ChatGPT (versions 3.5 and 4) to generate code solutions
Executing the generated code on a local machine and comparing outputs to expected results
Analyzing the correctness, readability, and structure of ChatGPT-generated solutions
problems: Evaluating the effectiveness of ChatGPT in generating correct solutions for undergraduate Java programming exercises
Assessing the potential and limitations of ChatGPT as a tool for students in programming education
Identifying risks of misuse of ChatGPT as a cheating aid in programming assessments
datasets: 80 diverse programming exercises from an undergraduate Java programming course

results.value: ChatGPT accurately generates Java programming solutions for most exercises
Generated solutions are highly readable and well-structured
ChatGPT can produce alternative, memory-efficient solutions
ChatGPT struggles and often produces invalid solutions

novelty_claims: First systematic evaluation of ChatGPT's effectiveness in generating solutions for a large set (80) of undergraduate Java programming exercises
Analysis of both strengths (accuracy, readability, alternative solutions) and limitations (handling non-textual/class file exercises) of ChatGPT in the context of programming education
Discussion of implications for educators regarding the design of coding exercises to minimize misuse of ChatGPT as a cheating tool
